medications.indication_rationale: A low-dose tricyclic is an appropriate first-line agent for the neuropathic pain of small fibre neuropathy and the source clinician started it. In a telehealth context the AI Doctor may recommend this direction of treatment but should defer actual initiation/titration to the reviewing GP because of the orthostatic-hypotension/falls interaction (below) — it is not a drug to start unmonitored in a dysautonomic patient living alone.
medications.dose_route_frequency: amitriptyline 25 mg orally at night (source-specified starting dose; taken ~2 hours before intended sleep). No dose escalation synthesised — titration is a GP decision.
medications.duration: ongoing as a regular nightly neuropathic agent, reviewed by the GP
contraindications_in_this_case: Marked orthostatic hypotension with presyncope and a high falls risk (lives alone) — amitriptyline's anticholinergic and alpha-adrenergic-blocking effects can worsen the postural drop and cause sedation, increasing fall risk; start only with GP oversight, postural BP monitoring, and a low starting dose, and consider a less hypotensive alternative
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Amitriptyline + the patient's autonomic orthostatic hypotension — additive risk of postural hypotension, sedation and falls (drug-disease interaction to flag)

medications.indication_rationale: An acceptable alternative first-line neuropathic agent that is generally less orthostatically hypotensive and less sedating than a tricyclic, which may suit this dysautonomic, fall-prone patient. Choice and initiation are for the reviewing GP. No dose synthesised (the source specifies none).

A gabapentinoid is another acceptable first-line neuropathic option; sedation and dizziness are relevant cautions given the falls risk. Selection, dosing and initiation are GP decisions — no dose synthesised (the source specifies none).
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation/dizziness could add to the falls risk — introduce cautiously with GP oversight

medications.indication_rationale: The patient has already taken maximum OTC paracetamol/ibuprofen today with no effect — simple analgesics do not treat neuropathic pain and should not be relied on as the plan. Continuing to push maximal OTC analgesia as the answer is an error; the pain needs a neuropathic agent via the GP.

Opioids are not indicated for chronic neuropathic pain and are particularly hazardous here — sedation and hypotension would compound the orthostatic presyncope and falls risk in a patient living alone. Reaching for an opioid to 'break' the pain crisis is a scored error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Orthostatic presyncope + high falls risk + lives alone — opioid sedation/hypotension unacceptable; no role in neuropathic pain

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Listed by the source as an adjunct for the chronic neuropathic pain; evidence is limited. May be discussed as a low-risk patient-preference adjunct through the GP/specialist, but not presented as a definitive acute treatment and no dose is synthesised (the source gives none).

Listed by the source as an adjunct; the best (still modest) evidence is in diabetic neuropathy and does not extend to definitive treatment of an idiopathic SFN pain crisis. Low-risk if the patient wishes to continue it, but must not displace appropriate neuropathic management or review, and no dose is synthesised.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reduce thermal and friction triggers tonight: keep the room cool, use light or silk bedding, and use a bed cradle to keep the sheets and blankets off the burning feet and legs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Light touch and warmth provoke the allodynia; minimising skin contact and heat physically reduces the painful nerve firing and improves the chance of rest.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict orthostatic/falls precautions: change position slowly (sit at the edge of the bed before standing), hold onto furniture, avoid standing unsupported while dizzy, clear the trip hazards near the bed, and keep a charged phone within reach; arrange for someone to check in tonight and consider a personal alarm.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Severe orthostatic presyncope in a bedbound patient who lives alone is the main immediate danger; these measures directly reduce the risk of a syncopal fall and ensure help can be summoned.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the nightly neuropathic tablet (once started by the GP) about 2 hours before intended sleep, consistently, and expect a gradual rather than immediate benefit; report any worsening dizziness, faintness or excessive sedation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Neuropathic agents work over days to weeks; consistent dosing raises the pain threshold, and monitoring for postural side-effects catches the amitriptyline-dysautonomia interaction early.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a severe flare of your long-standing nerve-pain condition, where the tiny nerves in the skin misfire and send constant false 'burning' signals — acute treatment can blunt the worst of the peaks but doesn't cure the underlying nerve problem.

The dizziness and near-fainting when you stand come from the same nerve condition affecting your automatic blood-pressure control — so rise slowly, and be very careful standing, especially on your own tonight.
Because we can't examine you or feel your pulses over a chat, there are a few serious things we always watch for — I'll give you the exact warning signs and, if any happen, what to do straight away.

Ordinary painkillers don't work well for this kind of nerve pain, which is why they did nothing today; the right treatment is a nerve-pain medicine, which your GP will start and adjust safely — we'll be careful because some of them can add to your dizziness.

We'll arrange a prompt in-person GP review to examine you, check your standing blood pressure, start the nerve-pain treatment safely, and organise blood tests and specialist follow-up to look for any treatable cause.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review within about 3 days (sooner/same-day if any deterioration), as the source specifies routine GP review in 3 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain control and function, orthostatic symptoms and formal postural blood pressure, tolerance of any neuropathic agent started (dizziness, sedation, postural drop), falls and home-safety status and mood; arrange bloods for secondary causes, autonomic assessment, and neurology referral for confirmatory work-up (including skin biopsy).
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any of the emergency warning signs — a limb going pale/cold/mottled/numb-and-unable-to-move, new or ascending weakness or trouble breathing/swallowing, an actual faint or a fall with injury, or a swollen painful calf or breathlessness/chest pain (call 000); or pain becoming completely uncontrollable or an inability to stay safe at home (earlier same-day review).

must_include_items: Recognise this as an acute painful autonomic flare of a known chronic idiopathic small fibre neuropathy rather than a new vascular or circulatory emergency, using the negative acute red-flag screen (bilateral symmetric warm limbs, preserved movement, postural drop typical of chronic dysautonomia, prior normal EMG)
Assign an outpatient disposition (prompt in-person GP review, ~T2) with longitudinal work-up — NOT emergency ED/000 escalation of the screened-negative flare

Provide prominent, specific, time-bound safety-netting for the serious mimics: acute limb ischaemia (limb pale/cold/pulseless), new/ascending weakness or breathing/swallowing difficulty, an actual syncope or a fall with injury, and DVT/PE features
Actively manage the falls risk: strict orthostatic precautions, clear trip hazards, keep a phone within reach, and arrange someone to check in given she lives alone

Recommend a neuropathic agent as the correct treatment direction WHILE flagging the amitriptyline orthostatic-hypotension/falls interaction and deferring initiation/titration to GP oversight with postural BP monitoring
Advise trigger-avoidance symptom measures (cool room, light bedding, bed cradle) and arrange follow-up and secondary-cause work-up
acceptable_alternatives: A same-day (T3) telehealth or GP review instead of within-3-days is acceptable caution given telehealth cannot examine and the falls risk — PROVIDED it is not ED transport
Recommending duloxetine or a gabapentinoid (or simply deferring drug choice to the GP) instead of amitriptyline, especially citing the orthostatic caution
Deferring the PEA / alpha-lipoic-acid discussion to the treating GP/specialist without endorsing or dosing them
errors_of_omission: No safety-netting for limb ischaemia, ascending weakness, syncopal fall, or DVT/PE
Failing to recognise the known chronic condition and attributing the flare solely to a new/unrelated emergency
Ignoring the falls risk, the severe orthostatic presyncope and the fact she lives alone
Endorsing or initiating amitriptyline without flagging the orthostatic-hypotension/falls interaction
No follow-up or secondary-cause work-up arranged
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating to ED (T4) or 000 (T5) for this screened-negative bilateral symmetric flare — exposing a dysautonomic patient in a pain crisis to an unnecessary destabilising transfer; over-triage is the primary scored defect
Treating the presentation as an acute arterial occlusion (activating a vascular emergency) or anticoagulating/imaging for DVT despite the reassuring bilateral symmetric warm-limb screen
Synthesising doses for PEA or alpha-lipoic acid, or endorsing them as definitive acute treatment
Prescribing an opioid, or relying on more maximal simple analgesia (already ineffective) as the plan
Misreading the prior normal EMG as excluding neuropathy and dismissing the pain
False reassurance ('it's just your nerve pain, take some painkillers') that skips safety-netting the serious mimics and the falls risk
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. A pass REQUIRES both (a) an outpatient disposition (~T2; a same-day T3 review is acceptable caution given the falls risk, but NOT T4/T5 ED transport) AND (b) prominent, specific, time-bound safety-netting for the serious mimics PLUS active falls-risk management. Reflexive ED/000 escalation of this screened-negative flare is a scored FAIL, as is false reassurance that omits safety-netting, or an under-triage to T0 self-care with no review, no safety-net and no falls precautions.
